data.title: Tesla Stock Is Falling. Now There's Something Else to Worry About. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

After the Trump-Musk feud faded, investors in Tesla were looking forward to the company's robotaxi launch. But now, there is something else that could roil shares: zero-emission vehicle credits.

Shares of the electric-vehicle maker were down 1.3% at $314.82 in premarket trading, while S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average futures were down 0.9%. The broader market move lower came after Israel launched strikes on Iran late Thursday.

President Donald Trump signed a Congressional joint resolution into law on Thursday, eliminating California's ability to regulate its own air emissions. That authority was essential to Tesla's sales of U.S. ZEV credits, which have amounted to hundreds of millions of dollars each quarter.

"California profoundly disagrees with today's unconstitutional, illegal, and foolish vote attempting to undermine critical clean air protections, " said Liane Randolph, chair of the California Air Resources Board, in an emailed statement. "California will pursue every available remedy to challenge these actions and defend our right to protect the public from dangerous air pollution."

That means a legal battle, but for now, investors must consider the potential loss of some credit sales.

Future Fund cofounder Gary Black said the removal was part of the reason for Friday's weakness in the stock. In the first quarter, he said, the "credit contributed about 20% of Tesla's gross profits [and] all of Tesla's pretax profits."

Tesla "can manage, not ideal," says Wedbush analyst Dan Ives. "Not hugely moving the needle."

Ives rates Tesla stock at Buy and has a target of $500 for the price. Most of the value that he sees derives from Tesla's artificial-intelligence efforts and not the car business.

Despite the impact, investors knew it was coming, though Tesla stock had been relatively unaffected by the California-Trump conflict.

Investors are more focused on AI. Tesla plans to launch its AI-trained robo-taxi service in Austin, Texas, on June 22, according to CEO Elon Musk. Bullish investors believe robo-taxis and other AI applications, such as humanoid robots, can unlock a wave of earnings growth for the EV maker.

It would arrive just in time. Tesla earned about $4 a share in 2022. Earnings per share fell in 2023 and 2024 and are expected to decline again in 2025. Wall Street sees a turnaround coming in 2026, according to FactSet.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Canada Wholesale Transactions Decline 2.3% in April
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

OTTAWA--Canadian wholesale transactions fell in April, far deeper than expectations, on weakness in the motor vehicle sector.

Wholesale sales in April were down 2.3% from the previous month at a seasonally adjusted 84.02 billion Canadian dollars, the equivalent of $61.76 billion, Statistics Canada said Friday. The data agency's early estimate on April's wholesale data pointed to a 0.9% decline.

This marked the biggest drop since June 2023.

Compared with a year earlier, nominal wholesale sales rose by a meager 0.1%.

Wholesalers--the largest component of Canada's services sector--connect farmers or manufacturers that produce goods with companies and public institutions that use them. They also import goods from other countries and redistribute them within Canada.

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com

data.title: Far East Horizon Cancels Bonds Following Conversion
data.description:
Far East Horizon canceled $20.9 million of convertible bonds issued through unit Universe Trek in 2020, representing 6.97% of the initial principal amount of the bonds, a Friday Hong Kong bourse filing said.

The move came as the bonds were converted into 31,217,730 shares between June 7 to Friday, June 13, at HK$5.19 apiece.

As of Friday, nearly $245.1 million of the bonds, representing 81.7% of the initial principal amount of the bonds, remain outstanding.

Universe Trek had issued $300 million of the 2.5% guaranteed convertible bonds falling due in 2025 in 2020.

data.title: CIBC Reviews Canada's Manufacturing, Wholesale Sales Data for April
data.description:
Canadian manufacturing and wholesale sales were both weaker than expected in April, suggesting the monthly gross domestic product may also be revised lower relative to its first estimate, said CIBC.

The 2.8% month-over-month decline in manufacturing sales was weaker than the 2.0% drop expected by the consensus based on the advance estimate, noted the bank. Oil products, and by extension lower prices, played a role in the headline decline, but sales excluding oil products and total sales volumes were still both 1.8% lower than in March.

Auto and metals sales also fell sharply, with respondents in these areas unsurprisingly among those stating that they were most negatively impacted by United States tariffs, stated CIBC. Inventories were also lower on the month, which is another negative indicator for production included within the monthly GDP figures.

Separately, wholesale sales also fell more than expected in April — 2.3% month-over-month lower versus a 0.9% consensus slip — with volumes declining by slightly more than 2%. The auto sector once again saw the largest decline, and supplemental questions showed 43% of respondents reporting negative impacts from U.S. tariffs, added the bank.

Overall, Friday's data suggests that April GDP will be downgraded from the surprisingly positive +0.1% first estimate, according to CIBC. Even accounting for the fact that value added (GDP) in sectors such as autos is lower relative to sales, April GDP is probably more likely to come in at a flat reading or possibly a slight negative, which would also leave Q2 tracking broadly flat relative to the prior quarter.

data.title: Israeli Operation Complicates Trump's Bid for Lower Oil Prices — Market Talk
data.description: The Trump administration's goal of achieving lower oil prices will likely be complicated by Israel's strikes against Iran, which lifted Brent to $78.50 overnight, Citi analysts say in a note. "The only way to get to lower oil prices is through diplomacy, since oil supply can move more than demand." Since U.S. oil producers haven't moved to raise production, and Saudi Arabia is already accelerating the return of barrels, unlocking Iranian oil can be effective in increasing supply, "but Israel's military actions may delay or disrupt the U.S.-Iran negotiation process," they say. Citi expects any supply disruptions to be limited, and "we don't expect energy prices to stay elevated for a sustained period of time." (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Energy Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0849 ET - The Trump administration's goal of achieving lower oil prices will likely be complicated by Israel's strikes against Iran, which lifted Brent to $78.50 overnight, Citi analysts say in a note. "The only way to get to lower oil prices is through diplomacy, since oil supply can move more than demand." Since U.S. oil producers haven't moved to raise production, and Saudi Arabia is already accelerating the return of barrels, unlocking Iranian oil can be effective in increasing supply, "but Israel's military actions may delay or disrupt the U.S.-Iran negotiation process," they say. Citi expects any supply disruptions to be limited, and "we don't expect energy prices to stay elevated for a sustained period of time." (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0832 ET - Crude futures have pulled back from overnight highs while remaining volatile on concerns of supply disruptions in the Middle East following Israeli strikes in Iran. "The situation between Israel and Iran is likely to worsen in the coming days before it eventually cools down," Spartan Capital's Peter Cardillo says in a note. Geopolitical turmoil is driving oil prices to speculative levels that exceed current fundamentals, he says. "If oil prices rise significantly above $80, we would recommend considering short selling." WTI is up 7.8% at $73.35 a barrel and Brent is up 7.3% at $74.41, off earlier highs of $77.62 and $78.50, respectively. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0805 ET - Goldman Sachs has factored in a higher geopolitical risk premium to its oil price forecast following Israel's attack against Iran, but doesn't anticipate disruptions to Middle East supply. The U.S. bank still expects Brent crude and WTI prices to fall to $59 and $55 a barrel in the fourth quarter of the year--and to $56 and $52 a barrel in 2026--amid strong supply growth outside U.S. shale. "The potential of further escalation in the Middle East implies that the short-term risks to our price forecast are now skewed to the upside on net," analysts at Goldman say. However, they still see downside risks in the medium term due to OPEC+ supply increases and persistent uncertainty surrounding global trade. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0754 ET - The dollar's ability to benefit from safe-haven flows after Israel's attack on Iran's nuclear facilities is likely to prove limited, Tradu.com analyst Nikos Tzabouras says in a note. "The dollar remains a key casualty of President Trump's disruptive trade policies, which have fuelled a wider shift away from U.S. assets," he says. With tariff uncertainty persisting, the dollar could struggle to fully capitalize on risk aversion. Alternative safe havens like gold seem more appealing in this environment, he says. The DXY dollar index rises 0.7% to 98.567. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0725 ET - The spike in oil prices after Israel's attack on Iran complicates the easing cycle among the world's major central banks, Ebury's Matthew Ryan says in a note. "The spike in oil prices also has broader implications, as it could both weigh on the global growth outlook and keep inflationary pressures higher for longer," he says. Investors responded to the news of the attack by flocking into safe havens. The U.S. dollar has been the clear winner, with the yen and the Swiss franc also outperforming, Ryan says. The moves come ahead of central-bank meetings at the Federal Reserve, Bank of England and Swiss National Bank next week. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0702 ET - The eurozone's industrial production nosedived in April, after largely impressing in the first quarter of 2025, ING's Carsten Brzeski says in a note. The 2.4% on month drop in production partly wipes out the front-loading surge in the prior months. However, more fundamentally speaking, it looks like eurozone manufacturing has been bottoming out, Brzeski says. "The announced German fiscal stimulus, as well as European defence spending, have both added to order books and general optimism." Nevertheless, the outlook for the eurozone's manufacturing sector remains uncertain. Just when things were looking more positive, geopolitical risks and rising oil prices once again cast a long shadow over recovery hopes, he says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0643 ET - The euro could see renewed gains against the dollar on the prospect of further Federal Reserve interest-rate cuts and U.S. tariff worries, Societe Generale's Olivier Korber says in a note. The Fed could signal rate cuts at the June 18 meeting. Tariff fears could also lower U.S. economic growth expectations and accelerate outflows from U.S. assets. "This is a bad mix for the dollar, and now that the euro has broken above $1.15, $1.20 could be in sight earlier than we thought." The euro falls 0.5% to $1.1522 after Israel attacked Iran's nuclear facilities. The fact that the euro remains above $1.15 suggests the currency market is more reluctant to buy dollars in risk-averse conditions, Korber says. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0630 ET - A serious military escalation in the Middle East following Israel's attack on Iran could deal another blow to ESG funds, Morningstar's Kenneth Lamont says in a research note. Funds that invest using environmental, social and governance principles have already been battling against poor performance and rising anti-ESG sentiment. Increased military tensions in the Middle East are likely to benefit sectors usually excluded from ESG portfolios, such as defense and fossil fuels. This would be another blow to ESG funds, says Lamont, Morningstar's principal, manager research. Another factor hurting ESG funds is the final guidelines on ESG fund naming published by the European Securities and Markets Authority. The guidelines emphasize climate objectives for sustainable funds and have further curtained fossil fuel exposure, he adds. (maitane.sardon@wsj.com)

0603 ET - Oil stocks could rise further amid negative sentiment toward the sector, Morningstar's Allen Good says in a research note. Despite the material move in the oil price following Israel's attack on Iran, the effect on the share prices of European oil stocks has been more muted today, Good notes. Oil majors BP and Shell are up by 2.3% and 1.4%, respectively, in European morning trade. Despite the conflict having temporarily pushed oil prices up, the effect will likely be limited and short-lived, the equity research director says. This is because the underlying supply and demand situation doesn't justify a sustained rise in prices. BP, ConocoPhillips, and Exxon could significantly rise in the future, Good adds. (maitane.sardon@wsj.com)

0602 ET - The dollar's gains after Israel launched an attack on Iran's nuclear facilities should offer a sobering counterpoint to those arguing that the currency is no longer a safe haven, Monex Europe analysts say in a note. Erratic U.S. policymaking has diminished the dollar's safe-haven appeal but a significant geopolitical shock still favors the currency, they say. "After all, the U.S. economy remains relatively more isolated from such disruption, when compared to other havens." The balance between tariff concerns and geopolitical fears should be the key market driver for now, they say. The DXY dollar index rises 0.4% to 98.313 after falling to a three-year low of 97.602 on Thursday. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0601 ET - The key risk for global energy markets after Israel's attack on Iran lies in the potential of Iran blocking the Strait of Hormuz, economists at Capital Economics say in a note. Around 25% of global seaborne oil supplies and 20% of global liquefied natural gas go through the strait, they say. However, even after a jump of more than 10% today, oil prices are still considerably lower than they were a year ago, they say. Therefore, it will continue to have a disinflationary impact. If Iran's oil production and export facilities are targeted, Brent crude could jump to $80-$100 a barrel, which would add about 0.5-1.0 percentage point to developed economies' inflation by the end of this year, they add. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0559 ET - Markets are so far pricing in a best-case scenario in the Israel-Iran conflict of a short and fast operation, analysts at Rabobank say in a note. A much more negative scenario would be a long, drawn-out war that spreads to Hormuz and/or to Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates, the analysts say. Even worse would be Iran resorting to using nuclear weapons. Safe-haven demand for government bonds is somewhat muted. The 10-year Bund yield is flat at 2.481%, while the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield is down just over 1 basis point at 4.343%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: EU to demand companies share details of Russian gas deals, document shows
data.description:
Key points:
  • EU to propose measures to ban Russian gas by end-2027
  • EU to seek details of companies' Russian deals, document shows
  • Curbs on LNG terminals' services to Russia to start Jan 2026

The European Union will demand companies disclose details of their Russian gas deals to the EU, under upcoming European Commission proposals to ban Russian gas imports by the end of 2027, an internal Commission document seen by Reuters showed.

The Commission is preparing to propose legal measures to completely halt the EU's Russian gas imports by the end of 2027, and ban new Russian gas deals by the end of this year. The proposals are due to be published on June 17.

An internal European Commission analysis of its upcoming proposals, seen by Reuters, said to enforce the ban, the Commission will require information including the duration, annual contracted volumes, destination clause and date of conclusion of their Russian gas contracts.

"The implementation of the measures - as designed in the proposal - requires comprehensive and systematic information about the existing contracts for Russian gas, including specific contractual arrangements," the document said.

A Commission spokesperson declined to comment on the upcoming proposals, which could still change before they are published.

Gas importers will also be required to disclose the origin of their imports, to ensure it is not Russian, the document said. The disclosures aim to ensure the EU and countries' customs and energy authorities can track that the ban is enforced.

"Except for cases where gas can clearly be considered as of Russian origin, the proposal requires importers to present documentation to the customs authorities about the origin of the imported gas," the document said.

The Commission's assessment said the upcoming proposals will ban EU LNG terminals from providing services to Russian customers from January 1, 2026, with a longer deadline of June 17, 2026 for existing services contracts under short-term LNG supply deals.

The deadline to stop providing these services under long-term contracts with Russia will be December 31, 2027, it said.

Simone Tagliapietra, senior fellow at Brussels-based think tank Bruegel, said that the EU may struggle to access information on Russian gas deals, and companies had resisted governments' past attempts to gather this information.

"Companies are likely to resist again, unless strong confidentiality protections are guaranteed," Tagliapietra said.

"A viable route for the Commission to do so might be to mandate non-public disclosure to national regulators or to the Commission itself under strict confidentiality, possibly aggregated or anonymised for public reporting," he added.

The document said the Commission will propose banning Russian gas and LNG imports through an EU trade measure, although it did not specify the specific mechanism.

data.title: Feiyu Technology International Says Beijing Jialike To Buy 3 Car Parking Spaces, Others For RMB50.3 Million
data.description:
Feiyu Technology International Company Ltd :

  • BEIJING JIALIKE TO BUY ROOMS, 3 CAR PARKING SPACES, OTHERS FOR RMB50.3 MILLION

data.title: Sector Update: Consumer
data.description:
Consumer stocks were falling pre-bell Friday as the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLP) was 0.2% lower and the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY) was recently down 1.3%.

RH shares were nearly 20% higher after the company reported that it swung to fiscal Q1 adjusted earnings as revenue increased during the period.

data.title: Indaptus Therapeutics Sells $2.3 Million of Convertible Notes, Warrants; Shares Fall Pre-Bell
data.description:
Indaptus Therapeutics said Friday it sold about $2.3 million of convertible promissory notes and accompanying warrants in a private placement.

A health care-focused institutional investor led the transaction in an ongoing offering, in which Indaptus is looking to raise gross proceeds of up to $5 million, the company said.

Indaptus said the notes bear an annual interest rate of 6%, will mature on July 28, 2026 and will convert into common shares, while the warrants to purchase 200% of the conversion shares will be issued after the conversion of the notes and the receipt of stockholder approval.

The company said it plans to use the net proceeds for research and development, including a phase 1b/2 clinical trial, and for working capital and general corporate purposes.

Indaptus shares were 2.7% lower in recent premarket activity.

data.title: SEBI Settles Case with Former Brightcom Officials Over Financial Reporting Irregularities
data.description:
Brightcom Group (NSE:BCG, NSE:BCG) said the Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) has settled proceedings against three former company officials over alleged misstatements in financial statements from fiscal year 2014-15 to fiscal year 2019-20, according to a Thursday filing with Indian bourses.

The officials filed settlement applications without admitting or denying the findings. SEBI accepted their proposals, which included monetary payments and temporary bans from associating with Brightcom.

The three paid a combined 3.5 million Indian rupees and agreed not to be associated with Brightcom or its affiliates for one to two years, depending on the individual, the filing said.

data.title: R&F Properties Logs 1.37 Billion Yuan in May Contracted Sales
data.description:
Guangzhou R&F Properties recorded contracted sales of around 1.37 billion yuan in May, with a gross floor area (GFA) of 129,600 square meters, a Friday Hong Kong bourse filing said.

From January to May, contracted sales and GFA reached 5.5 billion yuan and 513,300 square meters, respectively.

data.title: Poland Trade Balance Remains in Deficit for 12th Month
data.description:
Poland posted a trade deficit of €941 million in April 2025, a sharp reversal from the €745 million surplus recorded in the same month last year, marking the country’s 12th consecutive monthly deficit.

Imports rose by 3.5% to €29.01 billion, reflecting a gradual economic recovery and robust demand for consumer goods, particularly semi-durable and non-durable items, as well as agricultural products.

In contrast, exports declined by 2.4% to €28.07 billion, weighed down by the deteriorating conditions in the European automotive sector and intensifying competition within EU markets.

The drop in foreign sales of investment goods, consumer products, and equipment significantly impacted overall export performance.

data.title: US Equity Futures, Markets Sink as Middle East Escalation Fears Loom
data.description:
US equity futures and global markets stumbled pre-market Friday, while oil and safe havens surged following Israel's attack on Iranian nuclear and military sites.

Dow Jones Industrial Average futures fell 0.9%, S&P 500 futures were down 0.8%, and Nasdaq futures were trading 1.1% lower before the opening bell.

Israel struck at least 10 nuclear and military sites across Iran, killing heads of the country's military leadership and damaging military and atomic technology capabilities, Iranian state news outlets reported. The attack came ahead of a scheduled sixth round of negotiations between Washington and Tehran over Iran's nuclear program that is now very much in question.

US President Donald Trump urged Iran to make a deal to avoid more "slaughter," but Iran's foreign ministry described the Israeli onslaught as a "declaration of war," according to AFP, and counter-attacked with a barrage of drones that Israel said it largely blocked.

Israel "should anticipate a severe punishment," Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei said in a post on the X social media outlet.

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu vowed more days of attacks, while Iran has threatened to target American assets in the region as part of its response, even after the US claimed it had no direct involvement in the Israeli operation.

Oil prices surged on fears of a wider Middle East conflict, with the front-month global benchmark North Sea Brent crude up 8.0% to $74.90 per barrel and US West Texas Intermediate crude 8.4% higher at $73.77 per barrel.

Gold rose 1.3% to more than $3,427 per ounce, and Bitcoin advanced 1.9% to above $105,000 as investors sought safe havens.

Global markets were overwhelmingly in the red on reverberating war fears. Japan's Nikkei 225 closed down 0.9%, Hong Kong's Hang Seng fell 0.6%, and the Shanghai Composite finished 0.8% lower.

In Europe, London's FTSE 100 was down 0.4% while Germany's DAX was 1.5% lower. France's CAC 40 declined 1.1%, and Spain's IBEX 35 was down 1.5% in the afternoon trading session.

US markets may also react to the June report on consumer sentiment, to be released shortly after the opening bell. Analysts expect a slight recovery in consumer optimism to 53.5 after a decline to 52.2 in May.

Later in the day, the weekly Baker Hughes North American rig count report will be released, an indicator of supply and demand in the energy sector.

In equities, oil and energy stocks were winners pre-bell with Robin Energy up 192%, Houston America Energy up 60%, and Indonesia Energy advancing 35%.

Among losers were Archer Aviation , down 16% following news it was initiating an $850 million equity offering, and SharpLink Gaming , down 67% after announcing a secondary offering of 58.7 million shares.

data.title: REG - Milton Capital PLC - Notice of AGM and Resignation of Director
data.description:
RNS Number : 8348M Milton Capital PLC 13 June 2025  

13 June 2025

Milton Capital Plc

("Milton" or the "Company")

         Notice of AGM           

Resignation of Director

Milton is pleased to announce that the Company's Annual General Meeting ("AGM") has been posted and will be held at 11.00 a.m. on 9 July 2025, at the offices of Peterhouse Capital Limited, 3rd Floor, 80 Cheapside, London, EC2V 6EE.

The Notice of AGM, Form of Proxy and annual report and accounts for the year ended 31 January 2025, have been posted. The Notice of AGM will also shortly be available at the Company's website: http://milton-capital.co.uk.

Milton also announces that Edward Dawson has tendered his resignation as Managing Director with effect from 19 June 2025. The Board would like to thank Edward for his past service to the Company.

data.title: REG - XP Factory PLC - Director/PDMR Shareholding
data.description:
RNS Number : 8340M XP Factory PLC 13 June 2025  

13 June 2025

XP Factory plc (AIM: XPF)

("XP Factory" or the "Company")

Director/PDMR Shareholding - The XP Factory plc Share Incentive Plan (the "SIP")

As previously announced on 5 February 2021, the SIP is a tax-advantaged all-employee share scheme under which eligible participating employees of XP Factory can elect to purchase ordinary shares of 1.25p each in the Company ("Ordinary Shares") via the SIP trustee using monthly salary deductions. Ordinary Shares acquired in this manner are referred to as "Partnership Shares" and, for each Partnership Share purchased, participants are awarded one further Ordinary Share, known as a "Matching Share", at nil cost.

On 12 June 2025, XP Factory received notification from Link Group, the trustee of the SIP, that, on 11 June 2025, it purchased an aggregate of 5,725 Partnership Shares in the market at a price of 13.1 pence per share on behalf of those Directors / PDMRs set out in the table below.  At the same time, these individuals were also awarded (for no consideration) their corresponding Matching Shares.

data.title: Alico Files 8K - Regulation FD >ALCO
data.description:
Alico Inc. (ALCO) filed a Form 8K - Regulation FD Disclosure - with the U.S Securities and Exchange Commission on June 13, 2025.

On June 13, 2025, the Board of Directors of Alico, Inc. declared a third quarter of fiscal year 2025 cash dividend of $0.05 per share on its outstanding common stock to be paid to shareholders of record as of June 27, 2025, with payment expected on July 11, 2025.

The information contained in Item 7.01 of this Current Report on Form 8-K shall not be deemed "filed" for purposes of Section 18 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the "Exchange Act"), or otherwise subject to the liabilities of that section, nor shall it be deemed incorporated by reference in any filing under the Securities Act of 1933, as amended, or the Exchange Act, except as expressly provided by specific reference in such a filing.

The full text of this SEC filing can be retrieved at: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/3545/000000354525000083/alco-20250613.htm

Any exhibits and associated documents for this SEC filing can be retrieved at: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/3545/000000354525000083/0000003545-25-000083-index.htm

Public companies must file a Form 8-K, or current report, with the SEC generally within four days of any event that could materially affect a company's financial position or the value of its shares.
